 {"id":"bd-10x1","title":"Property Tests: bubbletea message routing","description":"Property-based tests for bubbletea message handling invariants.

## Purpose
Use proptest to verify message routing, batching,
and sequencing invariants.

## Properties to Test

### Message Type System
- [ ] Message::is::<T>() matches original type
- [ ] downcast succeeds for correct type
- [ ] downcast fails for wrong type
- [ ] Clone produces equal message

### Batch Execution
- [ ] All commands in batch execute
- [ ] Batch of n produces n results
- [ ] Empty batch produces empty result
- [ ] Nested batches flatten correctly

### Sequence Execution
- [ ] Commands execute in order
- [ ] Later commands see earlier results
- [ ] Empty sequence produces empty
- [ ] Sequence of 1 equals that command

### Command Composition
- [ ] batch(sequence(a,b), c) executes a,b,c
- [ ] sequence(batch(a,b), c) executes (a||b),c
- [ ] Deeply nested compositions work

### Model Update Loop
- [ ] update() called for each message
- [ ] Cmd results become new messages
- [ ] No messages lost in routing

## Test Strategy

```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn batch_executes_all_commands(
        cmd_count in 1usize..20,
    ) {
        let counter = Arc::new(AtomicUsize::new(0));
        let cmds: Vec<_> = (0..cmd_count)
            .map(|_| {
                let c = counter.clone();
                Cmd::new(move || { c.fetch_add(1, Ordering::SeqCst); Message::none() })
            })
            .collect();
        let batch = Cmd::batch(cmds);
        execute_cmd(batch);
        assert_eq!(counter.load(Ordering::SeqCst), cmd_count);
    }
}
```

## Success Criteria
- Message routing always correct
- Composition laws hold
- No lost or duplicate messages","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T18:56:51.016239622Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T18:58:03.951690725Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bubbletea","property","proptest","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10x1","depends_on_id":"bd-1u1s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T18:58:03.951653255Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-10x1","depends_on_id":"bd-ndn1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T18:56:51.050012361Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-114p","title":"demo_showcase: advanced interactions (mouse/resize/focus/paste)","description":"Make the demo feel premium by fully supporting advanced terminal interactions:
- mouse (click/scroll)
- resize responsiveness
- focus/blur awareness
- bracketed paste

Why:
- These are the “paper cuts” that distinguish toy TUIs from production ones.
- Supporting them forces correctness in the bubbletea event loop and component integration.

Must support (minimum):
- Mouse:
  - enable mouse mode in the Program
  - scroll wheel maps naturally to focused viewport/list/table
  - clicks focus/select (and enable obvious affordances on clickable UI)
- Resize:
  - handle resize events and recompute layout
  - preserve selection/scroll positions as best-effort
- Focus:
  - clearly visible focus state (inputs, query bars, panes)
  - consistent focus traversal
- Bracketed paste:
  - paste into search bars/forms without exploding into per-keystroke noise
  - “paste-aware” update logic (treat as single input chunk)

Acceptance:
- Mouse scroll works in viewports/lists/tables without fighting keyboard navigation.
- Resize reflows the UI and keeps it usable.
- Focus state is obvious everywhere.
- Paste works in search/forms and is visibly robust.

Testing:
- Unit tests:
  - focus traversal rules
  - bracketed paste chunk handling
  - resize layout breakpoints (given width/height)
- E2E:
  - docs scenario validates resize reflow
  - where feasible, add E2E injection for mouse wheel sequences (xterm) to prove scroll mapping.

Notes:
- Always degrade gracefully in headless/CI environments.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:06:09.027017204Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T23:43:10.181833548Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","input"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-114p","depends_on_id":"bd-yqh4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:06:09.040330682Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

-{"id":"bd-11zs","title":"Property Tests: lipgloss dimension calculations","description":"Property-based tests for lipgloss width/height calculations.

## Purpose
Use proptest to verify dimension calculation invariants
across all style combinations.

## Properties to Test

### Width Invariants
- [ ] output_width >= content_width (when width set)
- [ ] output_width == width when width > content_width
- [ ] padding adds exactly padding_left + padding_right
- [ ] margin adds exactly margin_left + margin_right

### Height Invariants
- [ ] output_height >= content_lines
- [ ] padding adds exactly padding_top + padding_bottom
- [ ] border adds exactly 2 (top + bottom) when present

### Unicode Width
- [ ] Wide chars (CJK) count as 2
- [ ] Combining chars count as 0
- [ ] Emoji width handled correctly
- [ ] ANSI sequences don't affect width

### Composition
- [ ] inherit() preserves dimension calculations
- [ ] copy() produces identical dimensions
- [ ] nested styles compute correctly

## Test Strategy

```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn width_never_less_than_content(
        content in "[\\w\\s]{0,100}",
        width in 0usize..200,
        padding in 0usize..20,
    ) {
        let style = Style::new()
            .width(width)
            .padding_horizontal(padding);
        let output = style.render(&content);
        assert!(visual_width(&output) >= visual_width(&content));
    }
}
```

## Success Criteria
- Dimensions always calculated correctly
- Unicode edge cases handled
- No panics for any style combination","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T18:56:06.723939917Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T18:57:57.783777937Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["lipgloss","property","proptest","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-11zs","depends_on_id":"bd-3m5y","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T18:57:57.783744084Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-11zs","depends_on_id":"bd-ndn1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T18:56:06.743725346Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-15ip","title":"glamour: Handle extreme table width edge cases gracefully","description":"# Task: Handle Extreme Table Width Edge Cases

## Problem Statement
In table.rs, the width calculation can produce degenerate results when
max_table_width is very small:

```rust
// Lines 505-550
let total_padding = column_count * config.cell_padding * 2;
let total_borders = (column_count + 1) * config.border_width;
let fixed_overhead = total_padding + total_borders;
let available_content = config.max_table_width.saturating_sub(fixed_overhead);
```

When max_table_width < fixed_overhead:
- available_content = 0
- All columns get min_width or 0
- Table becomes unreadable or panics on render

## Example Scenarios
1. Terminal width: 20, Table columns: 10, Padding: 1, Border: 1
   - fixed_overhead = 10*2 + 11 = 31
   - available_content = 20 - 31 = 0 (saturated)
   - Each column gets 0 width

2. min_width: 3, columns: 100, max_width: 50
   - Required: 300 chars minimum
   - Available: 50 chars
   - Result: Severely truncated, potentially invalid

## Solution Design

### Validation and Fallback
```rust
fn calculate_column_widths(
    table: &Table,
    config: &TableConfig,
) -> Result<Vec<usize>, TableError> {
    let column_count = table.column_count();
    let fixed_overhead = calculate_overhead(column_count, config);
    
    // Check if table can fit at all
    if config.max_table_width > 0 && config.max_table_width < fixed_overhead {
        // Table overhead alone exceeds max width
        // Options:
        // 1. Return error
        // 2. Reduce padding/borders
        // 3. Use minimum viable rendering
        return Err(TableError::InsufficientWidth {
            required: fixed_overhead,
            available: config.max_table_width,
        });
    }
    
    let available_content = config.max_table_width.saturating_sub(fixed_overhead);
    let min_required = column_count * config.min_column_width;
    
    if available_content < min_required {
        // Not enough space for minimum columns
        // Reduce to fit or error
        tracing::warn!(
            "Table content requires {} chars but only {} available",
            min_required,
            available_content
        );
    }
    
    // Continue with normal calculation...
}
```

### Graceful Degradation Options
1. **Reduce padding**: Set padding to 0 when space is tight
2. **Remove borders**: Use borderless style when constrained
3. **Horizontal scroll**: Indicate content continues (not visible)
4. **Column subset**: Only show first N columns that fit
5. **Wrap content**: Allow multi-line cells

## Implementation Steps
1. Add validation at start of width calculation
2. Define minimum viable table (1 char per column)
3. Add fallback rendering mode for tight spaces
4. Log warnings for degraded rendering
5. Add tests for edge cases

## Testing Strategy
```rust
#[test]
fn table_handles_narrow_width() {
    let table = Table::new(vec![
        vec!["Col1", "Col2", "Col3"],
        vec!["Data", "Data", "Data"],
    ]);
    
    let config = TableConfig {
        max_table_width: 10,  // Very narrow!
        ..Default::default()
    };
    
    // Should not panic
    let result = render_table(&table, &config);
    
    // Should produce some output (possibly degraded)
    assert!(!result.is_empty());
}

#[test]
fn table_width_zero() {
    let table = Table::new(vec![vec!["A"]]);
    let config = TableConfig {
        max_table_width: 0,  // No limit
        ..Default::default()
    };
    
    let result = render_table(&table, &config);
    assert!(result.contains("A"));
}

#[test]
fn table_single_column_narrow() {
    let table = Table::new(vec![vec!["LongCellContent"]]);
    let config = TableConfig {
        max_table_width: 5,
        min_column_width: 1,
        ..Default::default()
    };
    
    let result = render_table(&table, &config);
    // Should truncate with ellipsis
    assert!(result.contains("…"));
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No panic with any max_table_width value
- [ ] Graceful degradation for narrow widths
- [ ] Warning logged for degraded rendering
- [ ] Tests cover width=0, width<overhead, width<min scenarios
- [ ] Documentation explains behavior for edge cases","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:22:39.261784345Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:22:39.345406214Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["edge-case","glamour","robustness","table"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-15ip","depends_on_id":"bd-8wlh","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:22:39.345365178Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-15xi","title":"Logs: copy/export + clear actions","description":"High-polish actions for the Logs page.

Why:
- Real ops tools treat logs as data you can *use* (export, copy, inspect), not just a scrolling wall.
- This is also a great way to demo bubbletea effect composition (batch/sequence) and "real product" ergonomics.

Actions to implement:
- Copy:
  - copy current selected line (if selection exists)
  - copy visible window (current viewport slice)
  - if clipboard integration is not available in all environments, fall back to writing to a file and show the path
- Export:
  - export the full log buffer to a file
  - output location should be deterministic/test-friendly:
    - in E2E/self-check mode: write under `target/demo_showcase_e2e/` (or a Config-provided artifacts dir)
    - otherwise: write to a sensible user-facing path (e.g., `./demo_showcase_exports/`)
  - prefer a stable plain-text format; optionally provide a richer styled export if feasible
- Inspect in pager (stretch):
  - open the exported log file in `$PAGER` using the shell-out flow (release terminal -> run -> restore)
- Clear:
  - clear the buffer (with a confirm if needed) and show a toast

Implementation guidance:
- Use `bubbletea::batch(...)` for "toast + log entry + state update".
- Use the shared shell-out action for pager integration (no ad-hoc terminal hacks).
- All failures must become toasts/log entries, never panics.

 {"id":"bd-17p2","title":"huh: Fix MultiSelect cursor position mismatch with filtering","description":"# Bug: MultiSelect Cursor Position Not Adjusted When Filter Changes

## Problem Statement
The MultiSelect field has infrastructure for filtering (filter_value field, filtered_options() method)
but does NOT handle cursor adjustment when the filter changes.

## Current Code Analysis

### Cursor is in filtered-space (CORRECT)
The cursor correctly operates in filtered-space:
- Navigation bounds: `if self.cursor < filtered.len().saturating_sub(1)` ✓
- Toggle selection: `filtered.get(self.cursor)` ✓
- View comparison: `self.offset + i == self.cursor` ✓

### Missing: Cursor Adjustment on Filter Change
There is NO code that:
1. Handles filter input (updating filter_value)
2. Adjusts cursor when filter changes
3. Keeps cursor pointed at same item when filter results change

## Example of the Bug
\`\`\`
Initial: options = [A, B, C, D, E], no filter
cursor = 2 (pointing at C in filtered-space, which = C)

Filter applied: "a" (matches A only)
filtered = [(0, A)]
cursor = 2 (STILL 2, but filtered only has 1 item!)

Bug: cursor points past end of filtered list
- toggle_current() → filtered.get(2) → None → no toggle!
- view() → is_cursor = (offset + 0 == 2) → false → nothing highlighted!
\`\`\`

## Root Cause
When filter_value changes (once input handling is added), the code needs to:
1. Remap cursor to track the same item (if it's still visible)
2. OR clamp cursor to valid range (if current item is filtered out)

## Solution Design

### When Filter Changes
\`\`\`rust
fn update_filter_value(&mut self, new_value: String) {
    let old_filtered = self.filtered_options();
    let current_item_idx = old_filtered.get(self.cursor).map(|(idx, _)| *idx);
    
    self.filter_value = new_value;
    
    let new_filtered = self.filtered_options();
    
    // Try to keep cursor on same item
    if let Some(item_idx) = current_item_idx {
        if let Some(new_pos) = new_filtered.iter().position(|(idx, _)| *idx == item_idx) {
            self.cursor = new_pos;
            self.adjust_offset();
            return;
        }
    }
    
    // Item no longer visible, clamp cursor
    self.cursor = self.cursor.min(new_filtered.len().saturating_sub(1));
    self.adjust_offset();
}
\`\`\`

## Implementation Steps
1. Add filter input handling to update() method (KeyType::Runes)
2. Add update_filter_value() method with cursor adjustment
3. Add adjust_offset() to keep cursor visible after filter change
4. Add filter display in view() (show current filter text)
5. Add Backspace handling to remove filter chars
6. Add Escape handling to clear filter

## Testing Strategy (Comprehensive)
\`\`\`rust
#[test]
fn multiselect_cursor_stays_on_item_when_filter_narrows() {
    let mut ms = MultiSelect::new(vec!["Apple", "Apricot", "Banana", "Cherry"])
        .filterable(true);
    ms.cursor = 2; // Banana
    
    ms.update_filter_value("a".to_string());
    
    assert_eq!(ms.cursor, 2); // Still at Banana (now index 2 in filtered)
    assert_eq!(ms.filtered_options()[ms.cursor].1.key, "Banana");
}

#[test]
fn multiselect_cursor_clamps_when_item_hidden() {
    let mut ms = MultiSelect::new(vec!["Apple", "Banana", "Cherry"])
        .filterable(true);
    ms.cursor = 1; // Banana
    
    ms.update_filter_value("c".to_string()); // Only Cherry
    
    assert_eq!(ms.cursor, 0); // Clamped to valid range
}

#[test]
fn multiselect_filter_then_toggle() {
    let mut ms = MultiSelect::new(vec!["Cat", "Car", "Dog"])
        .filterable(true);
    
    ms.update_filter_value("ca".to_string());
    ms.cursor = 1; // Car in filtered list
    
    ms.toggle_current();
    assert!(ms.selected.contains(&1)); // Original index of Car
}

#[test]
fn multiselect_filter_navigation_bounds() {
    let mut ms = MultiSelect::new(vec!["A", "B", "C", "D", "E"]);
    ms.update_filter_value("a".to_string()); // Only A
    ms.cursor = 0;
    
    // Down should not move past end
    let filtered = ms.filtered_options();
    if ms.cursor < filtered.len().saturating_sub(1) {
        ms.cursor += 1;
    }
    assert_eq!(ms.cursor, 0); // Still 0, only 1 item
}

 {"id":"bd-2pex","title":"bubbletea: Command Execution & Error Handling Improvements","description":"# Epic: bubbletea Command Execution & Error Handling

## Background
The bubbletea crate implements an Elm-architecture TUI framework. The deep review
identified several issues in the command execution and error handling subsystems.

## Issues Identified

### 1. Unbounded Thread Spawning for Batch Commands - MEDIUM
**Location**: Lines 834-841 in program.rs
```rust
for cmd in batch.0 {
    let tx_clone = tx.clone();
    thread::spawn(move || { ... });  // One thread per command!
}
```
**Problem**: Batch commands create one OS thread per command with no pooling or limits.
A batch of 1000 commands spawns 1000 threads.
**Impact**: Potential thread exhaustion, high OS overhead.

### 2. Silent Channel Send Failures - MEDIUM  
**Location**: Lines 610, 628, 680, 682, 687, 690, 838, 847, 852, etc.
```rust
let _ = tx_clone.send(msg);  // Errors silently ignored
```
**Problem**: When main thread panics/exits, spawned command threads continue
but their messages go nowhere. No logging or visibility.
**Impact**: Silent failure, commands execute but results are lost.

### 3. Async Batch Commands Not Tracked - MEDIUM
**Location**: Lines 1293-1303
```rust
tokio::spawn(async move { ... });  // NOT tracked by TaskTracker
```
**Problem**: Batch commands in async context use bare tokio::spawn() instead
of tracker.spawn(). They don't receive cancellation signals during shutdown.
**Impact**: Long-running batch operations may delay shutdown.

### 4. Sequence Commands Block Async Loop - LOW
**Location**: Lines 1305-1312
**Problem**: Sequence commands execute synchronously in async context,
blocking the event loop until all complete.
**Impact**: UI freezing during sequential operations. (Documented behavior)

## Design Philosophy
- Thread pools for bounded resource usage
- Graceful degradation on channel disconnect
- Proper task tracking for clean shutdown
- Clear separation of sync vs async paths

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Batch commands use thread pool with configurable size
- [ ] Channel disconnection logged at debug level
- [ ] Async batch commands properly tracked
- [ ] All changes maintain backward compatibility","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:18:03.646169864Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:18:03.669219588Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bubbletea","concurrency","reliability"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2pex","depends_on_id":"bd-gyzr","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:18:03.669165347Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-3lk7","title":"charmed_log: Fix double-lock race window in log() method","description":"# Task: Fix Double-Lock Race Window in log() Method

## Problem Statement
The log() method has a brief window where configuration could change between
acquiring the read lock (to format) and acquiring the write lock (to write):

\`\`\`rust
// Current implementation
pub fn log(&self, level: Level, msg: &str, keyvals: &[(&str, &str)]) {
    let inner = self.inner.read().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner());
    // ... format output using inner's settings ...
    drop(inner);  // Release read lock
    
    // GAP: Another thread could modify configuration here
    
    let mut inner = self.inner.write().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner());
    let _ = inner.writer.write_all(output.as_bytes());
}
\`\`\`

## Race Condition Scenario
1. Thread A calls log(), acquires read lock
2. Thread A reads formatter=JSON, starts formatting
3. Thread A drops read lock
4. Thread B calls set_formatter(Text), acquires write lock
5. Thread B changes formatter to Text, releases lock
6. Thread A acquires write lock
7. Thread A writes JSON-formatted output (but formatter is now Text)

## Impact Analysis
- **Low severity**: Configuration changes during logging are rare
- **Inconsistent output**: Single log entry could use mixed settings
- **Not a crash**: Just potentially confusing output

## Solution Design

### Option A: Hold Read Lock, Capture Writer Handle
\`\`\`rust
pub fn log(&self, level: Level, msg: &str, keyvals: &[(&str, &str)]) {
    // Single write lock for entire operation
    let mut inner = self.inner.write().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner());
    
    let output = self.format_output(&inner, level, msg, keyvals);
    
    if let Err(e) = inner.writer.write_all(output.as_bytes()) {
        self.handle_write_error(&mut inner, e);
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Option B: Copy-on-Read for Immutable Config
\`\`\`rust
struct LogConfig {
    formatter: Formatter,
    level: Level,
    // ... other immutable config
}

pub fn log(&self, level: Level, msg: &str, keyvals: &[(&str, &str)]) {
    let config = {
        let inner = self.inner.read().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner());
        inner.config.clone()  // Clone config snapshot
    };
    
    let output = self.format_with_config(&config, level, msg, keyvals);
    
    let mut inner = self.inner.write().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner());
    let _ = inner.writer.write_all(output.as_bytes());
}
\`\`\`

### Recommended: Option A
Simpler implementation, single lock acquisition, atomic operation.
Performance cost is minimal (write lock slightly slower than read).

## Implementation Steps
1. Refactor log() to use single write lock
2. Move formatting inside the lock scope
3. Handle error within the same lock scope
4. Ensure no deadlock risk (CallerInfo::capture happens outside lock)
5. Add concurrent test to verify atomicity
6. Benchmark to verify no performance regression

        for _ in 0..100 {

            logger1.set_formatter(Formatter::Json);

    t2.join().unwrap();

            !line.starts_with('{'),

    }

 {"id":"bd-3lwk","title":"charmed_log: Add configurable error handler for I/O failures","description":"# Bug: Silent I/O Error Discarding

## Problem Statement
Line 598 silently discards all I/O errors:
```rust
let _ = inner.writer.write_all(output.as_bytes());
```

When writing to stderr fails (disk full, permission denied, pipe closed, etc.),
the error is completely ignored. Critical log messages vanish without any indication.

## Impact Analysis
- **Audit Logs**: Compliance-critical logs may silently disappear
- **Debugging**: Error messages lost when most needed (during failures)
- **Monitoring**: Log-based alerting becomes unreliable
- **Forensics**: Missing logs create gaps in incident investigation

## Scenarios Where This Fails
1. Disk full on log directory
2. Log file permissions changed
3. Pipe to log collector closed unexpectedly
4. stdout/stderr redirected to closed file descriptor
5. Docker container stdout buffer overflow

## Solution Design

### Option A: Configurable Error Handler (RECOMMENDED)
Add an optional error handler callback:

```rust
pub struct Logger {
    inner: Arc<RwLock<LoggerInner>>,
}

struct LoggerInner {
    // ... existing fields ...
    error_handler: Option<Box<dyn Fn(std::io::Error) + Send + Sync>>,
}

impl Logger {
    pub fn with_error_handler<F>(self, handler: F) -> Self
    where
        F: Fn(std::io::Error) + Send + Sync + 'static
    {
        let mut inner = self.inner.write().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner());
        inner.error_handler = Some(Box::new(handler));
        drop(inner);
        self
    }
}

// In log():
if let Err(e) = inner.writer.write_all(output.as_bytes()) {
    if let Some(ref handler) = inner.error_handler {
        handler(e);
    }
    // Optionally: attempt fallback to stderr if primary fails
}
```

### Option B: Return Result
Not recommended - changes the entire API contract and makes logging
awkward (callers don't want to handle errors from logging).

### Default Behavior
If no error handler is configured:
- First failure: Print warning to stderr (if available)
- Subsequent failures: Silent (to avoid infinite loops)

## Implementation Steps
1. Add error_handler field to LoggerInner
2. Add with_error_handler() builder method
3. Modify log() to call handler on I/O failure
4. Add fallback-to-stderr option
5. Add tests for error handling scenarios
6. Document the error handling behavior

## Testing Strategy
```rust
#[test]
fn error_handler_called_on_io_failure() {
    let error_captured = Arc::new(Mutex::new(None));
    let error_clone = error_captured.clone();
    
    let logger = Logger::new()
        .with_writer(FailingWriter)
        .with_error_handler(move |e| {
            *error_clone.lock().unwrap() = Some(e.to_string());
        });
    
    logger.info("test", &[]);
    
    assert!(error_captured.lock().unwrap().is_some());
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Error handler callback mechanism implemented
- [ ] Default behavior documented (silent after first warning)
- [ ] Tests for various I/O failure scenarios
- [ ] Example in docs showing error handler usage
- [ ] No breaking changes to existing API","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:15:57.520589539Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:23:21.251959454Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T02:22:55.633760095Z","close_reason":"Implemented configurable error handler for I/O failures. -{"id":"bd-awkz","title":"lipgloss: Fix incomplete OSC sequence termination handling","description":"# Bug: Incomplete OSC Sequence Termination Handling

## Problem Statement
In lib.rs lines 403-411, the OSC sequence handling doesn't properly validate
the String Terminator (ST) format:

```rust
State::Osc => {
    if c == '\x07' {
        state = State::Normal;  // BEL terminator - correct
    } else if c == '\x1b' {
        // Handle ST (ESC \) - we see ESC, transition to Esc to handle the backslash
        state = State::Esc;  // BUG: doesn't verify \ follows
    }
}
```

## Technical Background
OSC (Operating System Command) sequences have format:
```
ESC ] Ps ; Pt BEL      (7-bit, BEL terminator)
ESC ] Ps ; Pt ESC \    (7-bit, ST terminator)
```

The current code sees ESC and transitions to Esc state, but:
1. Doesn't verify the next character is \
2. If the next char is [, it would incorrectly start a new CSI sequence
3. Incomplete ST at end of string leaves state in Esc

## Example Malformed Input
```

```
Current behavior: After seeing \x1b in OSC, transitions to Esc, then [ transitions to Csi.
The "0;title" is ignored, and "[31m" is treated as a CSI sequence.

This is actually somewhat correct behavior (recovering from malformed input),
but we should explicitly handle this case rather than relying on state machine quirks.

## Solution Design

### Option A: Strict ST Validation
Add a dedicated state for "seen ESC in OSC, expecting \":
```rust

        state = State::Normal;
    } else if c == '\x1b' {
        state = State::OscEsc;
    }
}

State::OscEsc => {
    if c == '\\' {
        state = State::Normal;  // Valid ST
    } else if c == '[' {
        state = State::Csi;     // Malformed OSC, start of new CSI
    } else {
        state = State::Esc;     // Other escape sequence
    }
}
```

### Option B: Lenient Recovery (RECOMMENDED)
Keep current behavior but document it explicitly:
```rust

    // OSC sequence: ESC ] Ps ; Pt (BEL | ESC \)
    if c == '\x07' {
        // BEL terminator
        state = State::Normal;
    } else if c == '\x1b' {
        // Start of ST (ESC \) OR malformed input followed by new escape.
        // Transition to Esc state which will handle either case correctly:
        // - If next char is \, Esc state ignores it (simple escape)
        // - If next char is [, Esc state transitions to Csi (new sequence)
        state = State::Esc;
    }
    // All other characters are part of the OSC payload, ignored for width
}
```
